1: YHWH commands Moses to take Aaron and his sons with the vestments, anointing oil, and the animals for ordination. The divine initiative is the key theological point: ordination comes by divine summons, not human ambition. Hebrews 5:4: “one does not take this honor for himself, but only when called by God, just as Aaron was” — the legitimacy of priesthood rests on being called, not on self-promotion.
2: The materials of ordination include the priestly garments, the shemen hamishchah (šemen hammiššāḥ, anointing oil), the bull for the sin offering, and the two rams. The unleavened bread (maṣṣôt) connects the priestly ordination to Passover purity; the new priesthood begins in the context of redemption and sinlessness (1 Corinthians 5:8 “sincerity and truth”).
3: Moses assembles all the congregation at the entrance of the tent of meeting — ordination is a public, corporate act witnessed by all Israel. The public nature of priestly consecration is essential: those who serve the community before YHWH are accountable to the community (1 Timothy 3:2; Titus 1:6 “above reproach”).
4: “Moses did as YHWH commanded him” — the chapter's repeated refrain; Moses's precise obedience validates every subsequent step. No element is improvised; the entire ceremony is a replication of divine prescription. The pattern governs; creativity is not invited in the holy place.
5: “This is the thing that YHWH has commanded to be done” — Moses announces the divine authority behind the ritual before beginning. The declaratory formula grounds the ceremony in divine command rather than human tradition; the ordination's legitimacy is revelatory, not customary.
6: Moses washed Aaron and his sons with water — rachats (rāḥaṣ), washing/bathing; the water-washing is the first act of consecration, prerequisite to all that follows. Three elements mark priestly consecration: water (washing), oil (anointing), blood (sacrifice). 1 John 5:6-8 echoes a threefold witness of Spirit, water, and blood in the testimony to Christ; Hebrews 10:22 calls believers to draw near with “hearts sprinkled clean... and bodies washed with pure water.”
7: Moses clothed Aaron in the tunic (kuttonet), sash (ʾavnet), robe (mêʿil), ephod, and breastpiece, with the ephod's band tied around him. The vesting sequence invests Aaron with the symbolic clothing of office; Paul's clothing metaphor applies the same logic: “put on the Lord Jesus Christ” (Romans 13:14); believers are invested with Christ's righteousness as their priestly vestment (Galatians 3:27).
8: Moses placed the breastpiece (ḥōšen) on Aaron with the Urim and Thummim (hāʾûrîm wᵉhattummîm) — the priestly oracular objects; the exact mechanism of their use is unknown, but they mediated divine decisions for the community (1 Samuel 28:6). Christ as the ultimate oracular priest in whom “are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Colossians 2:3) fulfills the oracular function of the Urim and Thummim permanently.
9: Moses set the turban (miṣnepet) on Aaron's head with the tzitz hazahav (ṣîṣ hazzāhāv), the golden plate inscribed qōdeš laYHWH (“Holy to YHWH”). The high priest bore YHWH's name on his forehead before the divine presence, presenting it on behalf of all Israel. Revelation 22:4: servants of God will see his face and “his name will be on their foreheads” — the eschatological fulfillment of the high priest's golden crown applied to all new covenant priests.
10: Moses took the shemen hamishchah (šemen hammiššāḥ) and anointed the tabernacle and all its furnishings, consecrating them — the anointing of physical objects makes them sacred through the same oil that consecrates persons. The oil is the Spirit's material type; the Spirit's consecrating work makes what it touches holy. The tabernacle's anointing is the precursor of the Spirit's indwelling of the church-as-temple (1 Corinthians 3:16).
11: Moses sprinkled some of the anointing oil on the altar seven times (ševaʿ pᵉʿāmîm) — seven-fold sprinkling = complete and perfect consecration; the number seven throughout Leviticus signals fullness and completion. Christ's once-for-all sacrifice is the complete and final consecration of the true altar (Hebrews 10:14 “by a single offering he has perfected for all time”).
12: Moses poured the anointing oil on Aaron's head — yitzok shemen hamishchah al rosh Aharon (wayyiṣōq miššemen hammiššāḥ ʿal rōʾš ʾahărōn): “he poured the anointing oil on Aaron's head.” This act is the origin of the title māšîaḥ (Anointed One / Messiah); the anointing of Aaron's head made him high priest in perpetuity. Psalms 45:7 / Hebrews 1:9 applies the divine anointing “with the oil of gladness” to Christ; Acts 10:38 identifies Jesus as the one God “anointed with the Holy Spirit and with power.”
13: Moses clothed Aaron's sons in simpler vestments — tunics (kuttōnōt), sashes, and caps (migbāʿôt) — as YHWH commanded. The sons of Aaron are dressed differently from the high priest: a hierarchy of holiness and responsibility within the priesthood. In Christ, all believers are priests (1 Peter 2:9) with equal access, but Christ alone wears the full high-priestly vestments of perfect intercession (Hebrews 7:26-28).
14: Aaron and his sons laid their hands on the head of the bull of the sin offering (par haḥaṭṭāʾt) — even the high priest requires a sin offering for his own sins before the ordination proceeds. Ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin offering) is derived from the root ḥēt (sin) — the offering is literally the “sin-thing”; the animal carries the identity of what it removes. Hebrews 7:27: “He has no need, like those high priests, to offer sacrifices daily, first for his own sins and then for those of the people.”
15: Moses put the blood on the horns of the altar (qarnôt hammizbêaḥ) with his finger and poured the rest at the base — blood on the four horns purifies the altar itself; the instrument of sacrifice must be consecrated before it can accept offerings. The base-pour returns the excess blood to the ground; blood is YHWH's (Leviticus 17:11), and the earth receives what is not burned.
16: Moses burned the chelev (ḥēlev, fat) on the altar — the fat portions belong to YHWH in every offering (Leviticus 3:16 “all fat is YHWH's”). The fat represents the richest, best portion of the animal; offering the fat to YHWH is offering the firstfruits of life's abundance. The NT parallel: “offer your bodies as a living sacrifice” (Romans 12:1) — the best of oneself as the ongoing offering.
17: The bull's flesh, skin, and dung were burned outside the camp — the sin offering carcass is entirely destroyed outside the sacred precincts; nothing remains that might contaminate. Hebrews 13:11-13: “the bodies of those animals whose blood is brought into the holy places by the high priest as a sacrifice for sin are burned outside the camp. So Jesus also suffered outside the gate.” The burning outside the camp is fulfilled in the crucifixion outside Jerusalem.
18: The ram of the burnt offering (ʾêl hāʿōlāh) — ʿōlāh from ʿālāh (to go up); the entire burnt offering ascends to YHWH in smoke; complete consecration of everything to God, holding nothing back. The burnt offering has no portion for the offerer; it is entirely devoted. Christ's total self-giving: “who gave himself for us” (Titus 2:14), without reservation.
19: Moses threw (zaraq, zāraq) the blood against the sides of the altar all around — the vigorous dashing of blood stands in contrast to the careful finger-application of the sin offering (v15); different application methods signal different functions: the sin offering's finger-dab is precise and priestly; the burnt offering's blood-dash is complete and comprehensive.
20: Moses cut the ram into pieces and burned the head, the pieces, and the fat on the altar — the systematic total-offering: every part of the burnt-offering ram is consumed; nothing remains. The completeness of the offering models total consecration. The NT parallel is baptism as dying entirely to self and rising entirely to Christ (Romans 6:3-4).
21: Moses washed the entrails and legs with water and burned the whole ram on the altar — a reyach nichoch laYHWH (rêaḥ nîḥōaḥ laYHWH), “a pleasing aroma to YHWH.” The same phrase describes Christ's sacrifice in Ephesians 5:2: “Christ loved us and gave himself up for us, a fragrant offering and sacrifice to God.” The pleasing aroma language signals divine acceptance; the offering is received, not merely performed.
22: The second ram is the milu'im (millûʾîm, ordination/fillings) ram — a term unique to this ritual; millûʾîm literally means “fillings” or “investments”; it refers both to the filling of the priests' hands with offerings (v27) and to the complete investiture of the office. The ordination ceremony is a “filling” — the new priests are both invested and equipped, their hands literally filled with what they need for service.
23: Moses put blood on the lobe of Aaron's right ear (tenûk ōzen Ahărōn hᵉyāmānît), the thumb of his right hand (bōhen yādô hᵉyāmānît), and the big toe of his right foot (bōhen raglô hᵉyāmānît) — blood on the ear (to hear YHWH's commands), the hand (to do YHWH's work), and the foot (to walk in YHWH's ways); the whole person dedicated to YHWH's service through blood-consecration at every extremity. The pattern mirrors the cleansing of the recovered leper (Leviticus 14:14) — priest and healed-leper are consecrated by the same blood-to-extremities pattern.
24: Moses put blood on the right ear-lobe, thumb, and big toe of Aaron's sons as well — the entire priestly cohort receives the threefold blood-anointing; consecration is not only for the high priest but for all who serve in the sanctuary. 1 Peter 1:2: those who are “sprinkled with his blood” form the entire new covenant priesthood (1 Peter 2:9), all consecrated at their extremities through Christ's atoning blood.
25: Moses took the fat, the fat tail, the fat on the entrails, the liver lobe, the two kidneys with fat, and the right thigh — the choicest internal portions and the right thigh set aside as YHWH's premier portion. The systematic identification of the best portions for YHWH across all animal offerings establishes the theological principle: the first and best belongs to God (Proverbs 3:9 “honor YHWH with your firstfruits”).
26: Moses added to the fat one unleavened loaf, one loaf with oil, and one wafer from the basket of unleavened ordination bread — the grain offering (minḥāh) accompanies the animal offerings; bread + animal = the full human food-offering; both the labor of the field and the labor of the flock are presented. Christ as both the bread of life (John 6:35) and the Lamb of God (John 1:29) fulfills both dimensions.
27: Moses placed all these in the hands of Aaron and his sons and waved them as a tenufah (tᵉnûpāh, wave offering) before YHWH — the waving gesture presents the offering before YHWH and signifies his reception and return of what belongs to him. The priests' hands are literally “filled” (millûʾîm) with the offerings — hence ordination is called the filling of the hands; the priest is a mediating conduit between YHWH and the offering.
28: Moses then took the wave offerings from the priests' hands and burned them on the altar on top of the burnt offering — the ordination sacrifices are placed on top of the already-burning burnt offering; the ordination is built on the foundation of total consecration (burnt offering = total devotion). Ministry rests on prior whole-life dedication.
29: Moses took the breast (ḥāzeh) and waved it as a wave offering before YHWH — Moses's portion of the ordination ram, taken as the officiating priest's share. In this unique ceremony, Moses acts as the ordaining priest before the Aaronic priesthood exists; he performs a priestly function that only he can perform. The chapter marks a transition: the one mediator (Moses) installing the ongoing mediation of the Aaronic priesthood.
30: Moses sprinkled anointing oil and blood on Aaron, his sons, and their garments — oil + blood together; the dual element of consecration: oil (šemen, anointing/Spirit) and blood (dām, atonement) constitute complete priestly consecration. Hebrews 9:21 notes that Moses “sprinkled with the blood both the tent and all the vessels used in worship.” The new covenant antitype: Spirit and blood both applied to the believer-priest (1 John 5:6-8).
31: Aaron and his sons are commanded to boil the ordination flesh at the entrance of the tent and eat it there with the ordination bread — the covenant meal at the end of the ordination; eating in the presence of YHWH seals the covenant relationship begun in the ceremony. The priestly ordination meal is a type of the Lord's Supper as the covenant meal of the new covenant priesthood (1 Corinthians 10:16-17).
32: What remains of the flesh and bread is to be burned with fire — nothing of the ordination sacrifice may be taken away and profaned outside the sacred precinct; the completeness-of-devotion principle: what is given to YHWH is given entirely. The prohibition against leftovers prevents the holy from mingling with the common.
33: Aaron and his sons shall remain at the entrance of the tent for seven days (šibeʿat yāmîm), for it takes seven days to complete the ordination (milu'im) — the seven-day period corresponds to the seven days of creation; the ordination ritual creates a new order of priestly ministry. Only after the full seven-day formation period may the priests begin public ministry; preparation precedes service throughout Scripture (cf. Jesus's forty days of testing before his public ministry, Luke 4:1-14).
34: “As has been done today, YHWH commanded to be done lekapper aleichem” (lᵉkappēr ʿᵃlêkem): “to make atonement for you.” The entire seven-day ordination process is fundamentally an atonement-making procedure for the priests themselves; priests require atonement before they can make atonement for others. The Aaronic priesthood is structurally dependent on sacrificial atonement. Christ, by contrast, “offered himself unblemished to God” (Hebrews 9:14) — his ordination required no atonement because he was the sinless ordainer and the sinless offering simultaneously.
35: Aaron and his sons shall remain at the entrance day and night for seven days, performing YHWH's charge “so that you do not die” — the threat of death for deviation from the ordained process; the holiness of the divine prescription carries mortal weight. The death-threat is the measure of the seriousness of the encounter with the holy God. Hebrews 12:28-29 applies the same logic: acceptable worship requires reverence and awe because our God is a consuming fire.
36: “And Aaron and his sons did all the things that YHWH commanded by Moses” — the chapter closes with comprehensive obedience; “all the things” emphasizes completeness, leaving no element unperformed. The obedient conclusion validates the entire ceremony. Christ: “I have accomplished the work that you gave me to do” (John 17:4) — the same complete obedience that validated the new covenant ordination.
